# Interference and Diffraction

## Introduction
This experiment explores how light waves interfere when they pass through a double slit and compare with a diffraction grating.

- Use the observed fringe spacing to connect the pattern to wavelength and slit spacing.
- Record the wavelength setting used for each trial.

## Experiment Principle
Constructive and destructive interference produce bright and dark fringes whose spacing depends on geometry and wavelength.

- Equation cue: `d sin(theta) = m lambda`
- Figure cue: interference fringe geometry

### 2.1 Fringe pattern sketch
Insert or reference the sketch of the fringe pattern here.

## Experimental Procedure
1. Align the laser with the slit assembly.
2. Place the screen at the marked distance.
3. Adjust the slit width and capture the fringe pattern.

- Turn on the laser and verify the beam is centered on the slit.
- Move the screen until a clear fringe pattern appears.
- Capture one photo for each configuration.

## Required Observations
Observe how the fringe spacing changes when the slit width or screen distance changes.

- Note whether the central maximum is sharp or broadened.
- Record any misalignment or stray-light conditions.

| configuration | measured fringe spacing | notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 |  |  |

## Analysis Cues
Compare the measured fringe spacing with the theoretical prediction and explain any discrepancies.

- Use the table values to estimate the wavelength.
- Discuss whether uncertainty comes mainly from alignment or reading the fringe spacing.

## Thinking Questions
- Why does the fringe spacing shrink when the screen is moved farther away?
- What would change if the laser wavelength were different?
